name: Idioventricular Rhythm
identifyBy: Wide bizarre QRS at very slow rate 20–40 bpm
Ventricular escape - fires when all higher pacemakers fail
No P waves or complete AV dissociation
Irregular rate possible if multiple ventricular foci competing
Pre-terminal rhythm in severe bradycardia/cardiac arrest
management: Treat aggressively - idioventricular escape is a failing heart rhythm
Atropine 0.6–1.2mg IV - to increase rate of higher pacemakers
Transcutaneous pacing immediately if haemodynamically unstable
IV adrenaline infusion if pacing fails
Identify reversible causes: hypoxia, drug toxicity, complete AV block, electrolyte disturbance
Prepare for CPR - may deteriorate to asystole
pearls: Idioventricular rhythm is the last escape mechanism before asystole
Do NOT give lidocaine or other antiarrhythmics - will suppress the only remaining pacemaker
Pulsed idioventricular rhythm (with pulse) still requires urgent treatment - prognosis is poor without intervention
Complete AV block with ventricular escape: pace immediately
Agonal rhythm is a form of idioventricular rhythm with rate <20 bpm
differentials: Complete heart block with ventricular escape

qaNotes: Slow broad ventricular rhythm without preceding P waves.
Slow ventricular escape rhythm with dissociated sinus P waves marching through.
